questions.a: A majority of nodes required to make a decision.

questions.q: What is a leader in a replicated system?
questions.a: The node that coordinates writes or consensus rounds.

questions.q: What is consistent hashing used for?
questions.a: To spread keys across nodes while minimizing movement when nodes change.

questions.q: What is a vector clock used for?

questions.a: An operation that has the same effect when repeated.

questions.q: Why is exactly-once processing difficult in distributed systems?
questions.a: Network and process failures can happen between side effects and acknowledgements.
Nodes lose contact and both sides believe they should continue independently.

questions.q: Why do fencing tokens help in failover?
questions.a: They let only the newest leader act, preventing stale leaders from writing.

questions.q: Why is linearizability stronger than serializability?
questions.a: It also preserves real-time order of operations across clients.

questions.q: Why does 2PC block when the coordinator dies at the wrong time?
questions.a: Participants may be left waiting for a commit or abort decision.

questions.q: Why are retries dangerous without idempotency?
questions.a: A retried request can apply the same side effect more than once.

questions.q: When would you prefer eventual consistency over strong consistency?
questions.a: When availability, latency, or geographic distribution matter more than immediate agreement.

questions.q: When is quorum-based replication a good fit?
questions.a: When you want tolerance to node failures while still keeping reads and writes bounded.

questions.q: How would you handle leader failover safely?
questions.a: Use heartbeats, elections, and fencing so only the newest leader can write.

questions.q: When does consistent hashing matter most?
questions.a: When node membership changes often and you want limited key reshuffling.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with strong consistency across regions?
questions.a: Higher write latency and more sensitivity to network partitions.

questions.q: Why do systems use monotonic timestamps or logical clocks?
questions.a: To reason about ordering without trusting wall-clock time alone.
